![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

## Review

## Glucagon-like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists and Colorectal Cancer Risk

Jeremy W. Winkelman, MD 1, * , Matthew J. Levine, MD 2

- 1 Department of Internal Medicine, Scripps Clinic/Scripps Green Hospital, La Jolla, California
- 2 Diabetes and Endocrinology, Scripps Clinic/Scripps Green Hospital, La Jolla, California

## a r t i c l e i n f o

Article history: Received 12 January 2026 Received in revised form 20 February 2026 Accepted 22 February 2026 Available online 5 March 2026

## General Background

Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) is a chronic metabolic condition characterized by progressive beta-cell dysfunction and insulin resistance. It is one of the most common diseases worldwide affecting an estimated 589 million to 828 million individuals, including approximately one in 6 adults in the United States 1 . In addition to both microvascular and macrovascular complications, T2DM has been associated with increased risk of several types of cancers including liver, pancreas, endometrial, breast, bladder, colon, and rectal. Though there are many overlapping risk factors for both diabetes and cancer, including age, obesity, sedentary lifestyle, metabolic syndrome, and dietary patterns, there is

* Address correspondence to Dr Jeremy W. Winkelman, Scripps Green Hospital, 10666 N Torrey Pines Rd, La Jolla, CA 92037.

[E-mail address: Winkelman.jeremy@scrippshealth.org (J.W. Winkelman).](mailto:Winkelman.jeremy@scrippshealth.org)

## a b s t r a c t

Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a highly prevalent metabolic disorder and a well-established risk factor for colorectal cancer (CRC), mediated by hyperglycemia, hyperinsulinemia, chronic inflammation, and obesity-related metabolic dysfunction. Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs) are increasingly used for the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus and obesity, populations already at elevated baseline risk for CRC. This raises important questions regarding their long-term oncologic safety. This review summarizes mechanistic, preclinical, and clinical evidence evaluating the association between GLP-1RA therapy and CRC risk. Mechanistic studies largely suggest antitumor effects through metabolic reprogramming, suppression of oncogenic signaling pathways, inhibition of angiogenesis and epithelial-mesenchymal transition, immune modulation, epigenetic remodeling, and systemic improvements in insulin sensitivity and inflammation. However, real-world studies show mixed results. Many randomized controlled trials, meta-analyses, and large population-based cohort studies demonstrate a neutral association between GLP-1RA use and CRC risk. Several large observational studies report a protective association, particularly among individuals with obesity or those treated with insulin. In contrast, a smaller number of studies, including select randomized trials, retrospective cohorts, and genetic analyses, have suggested a potential increase in CRC risk, often attributed to detection bias or confounding variables rather than a clear causal mechanism. Overall, current evidence suggests that GLP-1RAs are unlikely to meaningfully increase CRC risk and may offer protective metabolic and anti-inflammatory benefits in select populations. Continued long-term surveillance and further studies are needed to further clarify this relationship. © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of American Association of Clinical

Endocrinology. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons. org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

evidence that diabetes itself contributes directly to carcinogenesis. This mechanism is through chronic hyperglycemia, hyperinsulinemia, insulin resistance, systemic inflammation, and oxidative stress. As a result, individuals with T2DM consistently demonstrate higher cancer risk 2 . Specifically, diabetes mellitus is a well-established risk factor for colorectal cancer (CRC), with some studies demonstrating a 30% to 40% increase in CRC incidence in someone with diabetes even after adjusting for overlapping risk factors such as obesity and physical inactivity. Multiple meta-analyses have reported higher risks for both colon and rectal cancer, and the association is more pronounced among individuals with longstanding diabetes 3 . A shared risk factor for both diabetes and CRC is obesity. Excess fat promotes chronic inflammation, alters gut microbiome, and contributes to metabolic dysregulation, all of which contributes to a pro-oncogenic environment within the colon. Individuals with obesity experience higher CRC incidence, worse oncologic outcomes, and greater postoperative morbidity 4 . A meta-analysis by Kim et al demonstrated a direct link between obesity and increased risk of gastrointestinal cancers. 5

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs) have become a popular treatment for type 2 diabetes and obesity. These agents mimic the actions of endogenous GLP-1, enhancing insulin secretion and suppressing glucagon in a glucose-dependent fashion, slowing gastric emptying, and increasing satiety. Since their introduction in 2005, GLP-1RAs have rapidly expanded in use due to their efficacy in glycemic control and weight reduction. 6-8

As these medications are increasingly used in populations at elevated baseline risk for CRC, understanding the potential impact of GLP-1RAs on CRC development has become a clinically important question. It has prompted a large number of studies investigating the long-term safety profile of these medications and whether there is any effect on cancer risk. A meta-analysis by Cao et al showed no increased risk overall of any type of cancer in type 2 diabetics who have been treated with GLP-1RAs 9 . Conversely, the real-world observation study by Yang et al showed through the FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) a higher risk of certain thyroid cancers, pancreatic neoplasms, and islet cell neoplasms 10 , and an observational study by Elashoff et al reported GLP-1RAs, specifically exenatide, were associated with an increased incidence of pancreatic cancer compared to other antihyperglycemic medications 11 . This review summarizes both the proposed biological mechanisms by which GLP-1RAs may influence CRC development and the real-world studies investigating this association.

## Potential Mechanisms Linking GLP-1RAs to CRC Risk

As data on the effect of GLP-1RAs on CRC risk is mixed, understanding the potential underlying mechanism is important. A variety of mechanisms have been studied, including metabolic regulation, intracellular signaling, immune modulation, epigenetic remodeling, and multiple, inter-related molecular mechanisms. These studies illuminate how GLP-1RAs could impact CRC development.

One mechanism involves altering tumor energy metabolism. One study demonstrated that the dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist tirzepatide markedly inhibited tumor growth and triggered apoptosis under both normal and hyperglycemic conditions. Mechanistically, tirzepatide reduced glucose uptake, destabilized hypoxiainducible factor-1 α , and suppressed glycolytic enzymes such as PFKFB3 and PFK-1. This decreased both glycolytic and tricarboxylic acid cycle activity. Of note, however, as tirzepatide exerts combined GIP and GLP-1 receptor agonism, these antitumor metabolic effects may not be fully generalizable to GLP-1 receptor agonists alone. Nonetheless, these effects suggest that GLP-1/GIP receptor activation disrupts tumor energy metabolism and could promote apoptosis in CRC. 12

Additionally, GLP-1RAs directly regulate tumor proliferation and survival through activation of the cAMP -PKA -AMPK axis and inhibition of multiple oncogenic signaling cascades including PI3K/ Akt/mTOR, ERK/MAPK, and epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) -signal transducer and activator of transcription 3 (STAT3). The CAMP-PKA-AMPK pathway would be expected to reduce cancer through various mechanisms including growth inhibition, antiinflammatory effects, enhanced autophagy, and opposing the Warburg effect by going from glycolytic to oxidative metabolism. mTOR complexes (mTORC1 and mTORC2) regulate translation of oncogenic proteins, epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), motility, and metastasis. The ERK/MAPK pathway activation promotes cell cycle progression, angiogenesis (via VEGF induction),

## Highlights

- Type 2 diabetes mellitus and obesity are independent risk factors for colorectal cancer through mechanisms including hyperinsulinemia, chronic inflammation, and metabolic dysregulation
- Preclinical and mechanistic studies largely demonstrate that glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists exert antitumor effects by suppressing oncogenic signaling pathways, inhibiting angiogenesis and epithelial -mesenchymal transition, modulating immune responses, and improving systemic metabolic effects
- Evidence from meta-analyses, randomized controlled trials, and large population-based cohort studies predominantly supports a neutral association between GLP-1 receptor agonist use and colorectal cancer risk
- Multiple large observational studies suggest a protective association between GLP-1 receptor agonists and colorectal cancer, particularly among individuals with obesity or those receiving insulin therapy
- Studies suggesting increased colorectal cancer risk are limited, inconsistent, and likely influenced by detection bias and confounding variables rather than a clear causal relationship

## Clinical Relevance

With growing use of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists for diabetes and obesity, understanding long-term cancer risk is essential. Available data largely support oncologic safety for colorectal cancer, though mixed fi ndings highlight the importance of continued surveillance, individualized risk assessment, and adherence to recommended colorectal cancer screening practices.

invasion, and metastasis through upregulation of matrix metalloproteinases and urokinase plasminogen activator. The EGFR and STAT3 signaling pathways promote proliferation, invasion, metastasis, and resistance to apoptosis, with EGFR activating STAT3 as a key downstream effector. These pathways converge to downregulate cyclin D1, increase p27 expression, and promote cell-cycle arrest and apoptosis. Studies using exendin-4 and liraglutide have demonstrated reduced ERK and mTOR phosphorylation, inactivation of GSK3, and activation of p38 MAPK, all of which limit tumor cell proliferation and enhanced programmed cell death. One study showed that exendin-4 administration increased apoptotic tumor fractions in CT26 colon cancer-bearing mice. 13-19

GLP-1RAs could also reduce angiogenesis and invasion. In diabetic murine colorectal carcinoma models, exenatide treatment lowered expression of angiogenic markers such as CD34 and Ki-67. These levels were reduced to levels comparable with cisplatin therapy, indicating an anti-angiogenic effect. 20

Other studies have demonstrated inhibition of tumor cell migration and invasion through modulation of the mTOR/p27/ RhoA axis and extracellular matrix remodeling. These agents suppress PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling and decrease expression of MMP-2, MMP-9, VCAM-1, ICAM-1, and VEGF. This upregulates matrix inhibitors TIMP-1 and TIMP-2 and tumor-suppressor genes including ITPR1 and ADCY5. This coordinated signaling restricts EMT and reduces metastatic potential. 14 , 21-24

In diabetic CRC models, dysregulation of the BMP4/Smad pathway enhanced tumor proliferation and EMT. Under high- glucose conditions, BMP4 and Smad1/5/8 activation were increased promoting invasion and metastasis. Treatment with GLP-1RAs reversed these effects by downregulating BMP4 and restoring GLP-1 receptor expression, leading to suppressed tumor growth and induction of apoptosis 25 . Similarly, studies using human CRC lines and xenografts demonstrated that liraglutide inhibited TGFβ /Smad3 signaling. This lowered TGFβ , p-Smad3/ Smad3, and N-cadherin levels while increasing E-cadherin expression, effectively reversing EMT. The liraglutide-treated mice showed significantly reduced tumor growth. 26

In addition to molecular effects, GLP-1RAs also potentially reduce CRC risk through immune and inflammatory responses. GLP-1 receptor activation promotes macrophage polarization toward an antitumor M2 phenotype, enhances natural killer cell cytotoxicity, and suppresses IL-6/STAT3 and NFκ B signaling, leading to lower production of pro-tumor cytokines. Preclinical models show that exendin-4 reduces oxidative stress and neutrophil extracellular trap formation. When combined with PD1 checkpoint inhibitors, the tumor suppression effect was further amplified. These fi ndings suggest that GLP-1RAs not only reduce inflammation but also alter the immune responses toward a tumor-suppressive state. 27-31

GLP-1RAs have been found to alter microRNA and DNA methylation patterns that control tumor-suppressor gene expression and EMT. GLP-1RAs downregulate miR-27a, which activates AMPK α 2 and induces cell-cycle arrest. Additionally, dulaglutide reverses hypermethylation of genes that help with epithelial integrity such as ESR1, CDH1, and ADAM33. Therefore, this hypermethylation can reduce invasiveness. 32

Analysis from The Cancer Genome Atlas studied the role of GLP-1 signaling -related genes across 33 cancer types and found evidence that GLP-1RAs could reduce CRC risk. They found that across several tumor types, GLP-1 pathway activity is generally reduced. In CRC specifically, low signaling correlated with worse survival and diminished immune infiltration. In contrast, higher GLP-1 pathway activity was associated with greater immune cell presence. In vitro, semaglutide inhibited CRC cell migration, consistent with this immunomodulatory and tumor-suppressive profile. 25

At a systemic level, GLP-1RAs could contribute to lowering CRC risk by reducing metabolic and inflammatory drivers of carcinogenesis. By improving insulin sensitivity and normalizing lipid metabolism, they lower the insulin/IGF-mediated proliferative signaling which is a central component of obesity-related cancer. Weight loss from GLP-1RAs also reduces chronic inflammation, decreases leptin levels, increases adiponectin, and enhances immune surveillance. Collectively, these all mitigate the pro-oncogenic effects of metabolic dysfunction. 32-34

Despite the majority of the mechanistic studies suggesting GLP1RAs could be protective against CRC, some mechanisms have been proposed as to how GLP-1RAs could increase CRC risk. One study notes that chronic endogenous GLP-1 overproduction during insulin resistance could activate Wnt/c-Myc signaling which would promote proto-oncogene transcription in intestinal epithelial cells 35 . Though this is not based in experimental data, it does propose a potential mechanism as to how GLP-1RA could induce CRC.

Current literature describes multiple potential mechanisms through which GLP-1RAs could inhibit colorectal carcinogenesis including metabolic reprogramming, suppression of PI3K/Akt/ mTOR and ERK/MAPK signaling, inhibition of angiogenesis and invasion, modulation of BMP4/Smad and TGFβ /Smad pathways, immunity enhancement, epigenetic tumor-suppressor mechanisms, and systemic metabolic normalization. Though the majority of these mechanistic studies suggest a protective effect, the real world data supports a more mixed picture.

## Evidence Supporting a Neutral Association

Many studies have demonstrated no meaningful increase or decrease in CRC risk associated with GLP-1RA use and can be summarized in Table 1.

A large meta-analysis of 90 randomized control trials (RCTs) conducted by Figlioli et al involving more than 120 000 participants found no association between GLP-1RA treatment and gastrointestinal or site-specific cancers, including colorectal malignancy (RR 1.13, 95% CI 0.92 -1.39). These fi ndings remained consistent across placebo-controlled, high-dose, and long-duration studies 36 . Similarly, a meta-analysis of 34 RCTs including over 50 000 patients with T2DM reported no increase in overall malignant neoplasms (odds ratio [OR] 1.04, 95% CI 0.94 -1.15) with null results observed for individual agents, including liraglutide, exenatide, semaglutide, or albiglutide, and results consistent in studies exceeding 3 years of follow-up 37 . A meta-analysis of semaglutide across 37 RCTs showed no increase in any type of cancer (OR 0.95, 95% CI 0.62-1.45), and analysis of 19 real-world studies showed only a single case each of pancreatic and B-cell lymphoma. 38

Some long-term individual trials also support a neutral stance. The LEADER trial was an international, phase 3b, randomized, double-blind, controlled trial primarily designed to evaluate cardiovascular outcomes. It followed 9340 adults with T2DM and high cardiovascular risk over a median of 3.8 years, and demonstrated no significant difference in malignant or CRC incidence between liraglutide and placebo groups (hazard ratio [HR] 1.06, 95% CI 0.90 -1.25) 39 . A pooled analysis of 26 RCTs ( n = 16 090) evaluating albiglutide, dulaglutide, and extended-release exenatide also found a neutral association (RR 1.02, 95% CI 0.74 -1.41), independent of drug used or treatment duration. 40

A neutral association was also found in some large populationbased cohort studies. A study utilized data from the UK Clinical Practice Research Datalink and identified 112 040 patients newly treated with antidiabetic drugs. They compared GLP-1RA or DPP-4 inhibitors to sulfonylureas in patients with T2DM and found no significant change in CRC incidence (HR 1.0, 95% CI 0.7 -1.6) and no duration-response pattern 41 . Another study utilized Medicare data between 2013 and 2020 to look at patients with T2DM on different antihyperglycemic agents who also were cancer naïve. They found no difference in CRC risk between GLP-1RAs and SGLT2 inhibitors (HR 1.03, 95% CI 0.95-1.12) or DPP-4 inhibitors (HR 0.96 [0.891.04]) 42 . A separate study using Medicare data from 2007 to 2013 also found no change in CRC risk when comparing GLP-1RAs to long-acting insulin use (HR 0.82, 95% CI 0.42 -1.58) 43 . A different cohort study used the OneFlorida + electronic health record, a multicenter health research network, and identified 86 632 adults with obesity and no prior cancer history. The results showed no difference in CRC rates between GLP-1RA users and nonusers (HR 0.88, 95% CI 0.64-1.22). It did show, however, a reduced risk of endometrial, ovarian, meningioma cancers, and overall cancer incidence (HR 0.83, 95% CI 0.76 -0.91) 44 . In total, these large data sets consistently show GLP-1RAs have a neutral impact on CRC risk.

One preclinical study found that when GLP-1RAs were administered to mice previously exposed to a colon-specific carcinogen, there was no increase in precancerous or neoplastic lesions 45 . A bioinformatics study examined the significance of GLP-1RA gene expression across thousands of tumor specimens representing multiple cancer types. It found no significant survival association in colon, pancreatic, liver, ovarian, or head and neck cancers. 46

In conclusion, there is evidence from meta-analyses, RCTs, large scale cohort studies, and preclinical studies that GLP-1RAs have a neutral impact on CRC risk.

Table 1

Studies Demonstrating a Neutral Association Between Glucagon-like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonist Use and Colorectal Cancer

|----------------|--------|----------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Figlioli 36    |   2024 | Meta-analysis of 90 RCT                                              | 124 791      | Placebo or other antidiabetic drugs | No association between GLP-1RA treatment and gastrointestinal or site-specific cancers, including colorectal malignancy (RR 1.13, 95% CI 0.92 - 1.39)                                                        |
| Liu 37         |   2019 | Meta-analysis of 34 studies                                          | 50 452       | Placebo or other antidiabetic drugs | No increase in overall malignant neoplasms (OR 1.04, 95% CI 0.94 - 1.15) in type 2 diabetes patients                                                                                                         |
| Nauck 39       |   2018 | International, phase 3b, randomized, double-blind, controlled trial. | 9340         | Placebo                             | No significant difference in malignant or colorectal cancer incidence between liraglutide and placebo groups (HR 1.06, 95% CI 0.90 - 1.25) in those over age 50 with type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular risk |
| Guo 40         |   2016 | Systemic review of 26 RCTs                                           | 16 090       | Other antidiabetic drugs            | No increased risk of tumor compared with other antidiabetic drugs (RR 1.02, 95% CI 0.74 - 1.41)                                                                                                              |
| Nagendra 38    |   2023 | Systemic review from 37 RCTs and 19 real-world studies               | 46 719       | Placebo or active control           | No increase in any type of cancer (OR 0.95, 95% CI 0.62-1.45)                                                                                                                                                |
| Abrahami 41    |   2018 | Retrospective cohort study                                           | 112 040      | Sulfonylureas                       | Use of GLP-1 analogues was not associated with colorectal cancer incidence (hazard ratio: 1.0; 95% confidence interval = 0.7, 1.6) in patients with T2DM                                                     |
| Lu 42          |   2025 | Retrospective cohort study                                           | 84 648       | SGLT-2i or DPP4i                    | Similar overall cancer risk in T2DM patients treated with SGLT2i (HR 1.03, 95% CI 0.95-1.12) and DPP4i (HR 0.96, 95% CI 0.89-1.04)                                                                           |
| Htoo 43        |   2016 | Retrospective cohort study                                           | 60 367       | Long acting insulin                 | Adjusted HR was 0.82 (CI 0.42, 1.58) between GLP-1RA users and LAI initiators.                                                                                                                               |
| Dai 44         |   2025 | Retrospective cohort study                                           | 86 632       | Non GLP-1 RA users                  | No difference in CRC rates between GLP-1RA users and nonusers (HR 0.88, 95% CI 0.64-1.22).                                                                                                                   |
| Kissow 45      |   2012 | Preclinical animal study                                             | 120 mice     | Water                               | GLP-1RAs were administered to mice previously exposed to a colon-specific carcinogen, there was no increase in precancerous or neoplastic lesions                                                            |
| Ungvari 46     |   2025 | Bioinformatics study                                                 | N/A          | N/A                                 | No significant survival association in colon cancers                                                                                                                                                         |

Abbreviations: CI, confidence interval; CRC, colorectal cancer; GLP-1 RA, glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist; HR, hazard ratio; OR, odds ratio; RCT, randomized control trial; T2DM, type 2 diabetes mellitus.

## Evidence Supporting a Protective Association

Several studies have shown a protective association between GLP-1RAs and CRC and can be summarized in Table 2. These studies include large scale cohort studies, large database sets, and meta-analyses, all of which demonstrate lower CRC incidence among GLP-1RA users compared with other antihyperglycemic agents.

A systematic review of more than 2 million participants across 5 studies, including prospective and retrospective cohort studies, found significantly lower CRC risk among GLP-1RA users compared with thiazolidinediones (RR 0.82, 95% CI 0.68-0.96), insulin (RR 0.57, 95% CI 0.32-0.81), and SGLT2 inhibitors (RR 0.77, 95% CI 0.590.95), with similar but nonsignificant protective trends against other comparator antihyperglycemic agents. 47

A large retrospective cohort analysis conducted by Wang et al using the TriNetX Research Network, which includes over 100 million deidentified patient records, analyzed 1 221 218 patients with T2DM and no previous CRC diagnosis. Using 1:1 propensity matching, the study found GLP-1RA therapy was associated with a significantly reduced risk of CRC in patients with T2DM relative to insulin (HR 0.56, 95% CI 0.44 -0.72), metformin (HR 0.75, 95% CI 0.58 -0.97), sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors, sulfonylureas, and thiazolidinediones. They found nonsignificant reductions compared with α -glucosidase and DPP-4 inhibitors. The protective association persisted across sex subgroups and was found to be strongest among patients who were overweight or obese (HR 0.50, 95% CI 0.33-0.75) vs insulin, 0.58, 95% CI 0.38-0.89 vs metformin). 48

A similar but broader study utilizing the TrinetX Research Network examined the association of GLP-1RA on 13 different obesity-related cancers, including CRC. It specifically compared GLP1RA users to those prescribed insulin or metformin. It found that GLP-1RA use in patients with T2DM was associated with a significantly lower risk for 10 of the 13 cancers compared to insulin, including CRC (HR 0.54, 95% CI 0.46 -0.64), but not compared to metformin for most cancers. The association remained consistent across age and sex subgroups 49 . Another TriNetX retrospective cohort analysis studied the effect of various weight loss interventions, including semaglutide, tirzepatide, and bariatric surgery, in diabetic patients who were overweight or obese. The primary outcome was impact on cancer development. The study used propensity matching and treatment with DPP4-inhibitor as a control. Of the 64 178 matched pairs, semaglutide users exhibited significantly lower rates of composite obesity associated cancers (HR 0.88, 95% CI 0.82 -0.95), including colorectal (HR 0.80, 95% CI 0.67 -0.97). The tirzepatide group (HR 0.84, 95% CI 0.69 -1.01) and bariatric surgery group (HR 0.85, 95% CI 0.74 -0.98) both showed a nonsignificant trend towards reducing obesity associated cancers. 50

A different retrospective cohort study utilizing the TriNetX Research Network propensity matched type 2 diabetics who were new users of GLP-1RAs to those who use other antihyperglycemic agents. They found that GLP-1RA therapy was associated with a significantly lower risk of gastrointestinal malignancies, including CRC, compared with other antihyperglycemic agents. The strongest protective effect was seen relative to insulin (aHR 0.29, 95% CI 0.23 -0.37), and similar reductions were observed against metformin, sulfonylureas, thiazolidinediones, DPP-4 inhibitors, and SGLT2 inhibitors 51 . These results suggest GLP-1RAs have protective effects against CRC. However, it is important to note a Letter to the Editor criticized this study for having methodologic biases. Specifically, they identify confounding by indication and time-lag bias as major limitations. 52

A large retrospective analysis of more than 680 000 patients from the Explorys database, validated using the FAERS, examined the relationship between GLP-1RA users and site-specific cancer risk compared with metformin users. Within 5 years of treatment initiation, GLP-1RA use was associated with a significantly lower incidence of colon cancer (adjusted OR 0.85, P = 0.03), as well as reduced risks of prostate and lung cancers. The FAERS validation showed similar trends, including decreased colon cancer odds and an exposure -duration effect. 53

|----------------|--------|----------------------------------------------------------|--------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|

Comparisons with sulfonylureas, DPP-4 inhibitors, and metformin were not statistically significant |
| Wang, L 48     |   2024 | Retrospective cohort study                               | 1 221 218    | Other antidiabetics drugs                                    | GLP-1RA therapy was associated with a significantly reduced risk of CRC in patients with T2DM relative to insulin (HR 0.56, 95% CI 0.44 - 0.72), metformin (HR 0.75, 95% CI 0.58 - 0.97), SGLT-2 inhibitors, sulfonylureas, and thiazolidinediones                                                                            |
| Wang, L 49     |   2024 | Retrospective cohort study                               | 1 651 452    | Insulin or metformin                                         | GLP-1RA use was associated with a significantly lower risk CRC in patients with T2DM compared to insulin (HR 0.54, 95% CI 0.46 - 0.64), but not compared to metformin                                                                                                                                                         |
| Ipaye 50       |   2025 | Retrospective cohort study                               | 128 356      | DPP4-inhibitors                                              | Semaglutide users with T2DM and were overweight/obese had significantly lower rates of CRC (HR 0.80, 95% CI 0.67 - 0.97)                                                                                                                                                                                                      |

The strongest protective effect was seen relative to insulin (aHR 0.29, 95% CI 0.23 - 0.37)                                                                   |
| Wang, J 53     |   2022 | Retrospective cohort study                               | 683 570      | Metformin                                                    | GLP-1RA use was associated with a significantly lower incidence of colon cancer (adjusted OR 0.85, P = 0.03)                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| Wu 54          |   2025 | Retrospective cohort study                               | 23 452       | Long-acting insulin                                          | Lower risk of CRC in patients with T2DM among the GLP-1RA group (HR 0.66, 95% CI 0.48 - 0.92) compared to Long acting insulin                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| Levy 55        |   2025 | Retrospective cohort study                               | 1 119 723    | Matched controls who did not receive GLP-1 receptor agonists | GLP-1RA users with obestiy demonstrated significant reductions in GI cancer risk (HR 0.67, 95% CI 0.59 - 0.75) compared with matched controls                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| Chen 29        |   2023 | Retrospective cohort study                               | 34 907       | Matched controls who did not receive GLP-1 receptor agonists | GLP-1RA use was linked to a markedly lower risk of all-cause mortality among cancer survivors (HR 0.36, 95% CI 0.25 - 0.51)                                                                                                                                                                                                   |

Abbreviations: aHR, adjusted hazard ratio; CI, confidence interval; CRC, colorectal cancer; GLP-1 RA, glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist; HR, hazard ratio; OR, odds ratio; RCT, randomized control trial; T2DM, type 2 diabetes mellitus.

A retrospective cohort study used nationwide data from a Taiwanese T2DM cohort. They identified 11 726 propensity matched pairs of GLP-1RA and long acting insulin users. The results showed a 34% lower risk of CRC among the GLP-1RA group (HR 0.66, 95% CI 0.48 -0.92), with the most substantial reduction occurring for rectal cancer (HR 0.53, 95% CI 0.28 -0.92). 54

A large retrospective study analyzed obese patients without diabetes and found similar results. It included more than 1.1 million adults with obesity from the TriNetX database. Over a 5-year follow-up, the GLP-1RA users demonstrated significant reductions in gastrointestinal cancer risk (HR 0.67, 95% CI 0.59 -0.75), compared with matched controls. 55

Relatedly, a retrospective cohort study at Northwestern Medicine evaluated 67 591 cancer survivors to determine whether GLP-1RA use is associated with cancer recurrence or allcause mortality. After adjusting for confounders, GLP-1RA use was linked to a markedly lower risk of all-cause mortality among cancer survivors (HR 0.36, 95% CI 0.25 -0.51), but not in the noncancer population. Similar benefits were seen in survivors of obesity-associated cancers. However, GLP-1RA use was not significantly associated with cancer recurrence in any group. Overall, GLP-1RAs were associated with improved survival but did not impact recurrence risk, highlighting their potential value in cancer survivorship care. 29

There is data from meta-analyses, large observational cohort studies, obesity-focused analyses, and survivor populations collectively supporting a protective association between GLP-1RA use and CRC. The protective effect appears most significant among individuals with obesity or those treated with insulin.

## Evidence Suggesting a Potential Increase in Risk

While the majority of studies suggest either neutral or protective association, there are some studies that suggest GLP-1RAs may have a positive association with CRC and can be summarized in Table 3. A meta-analysis conducted by Silverii et al of 50 RCTs lasting at least 52 weeks compared GLP-1RA use to other antihyperglycemic or anti-obesity agents. They found GLP-1RA treatment was not associated with a difference in overall cancer risk (MH-OR 1.05, 95% CI 0.98-1.13). They did, however, detect an increase in CRC risk (MHOR 1.27, 95% CI 1.03-1.57). This was significant in shorter term trials less than 104 weeks (MH-OR 2.28, 95% CI 1.04, 4.98) but not in trials over 104 weeks (MH-OR 1.20, 95% CI (0.96, 1.50). The authors attributed this to a potential detection bias due to increased gastrointestinal evaluation among GLP-1RA users rather than a true carcinogenic mechanism. 56

A different meta-analysis of 68 RCTs including over 207 000 participants was designed to evaluate whether GLP-1RAs or SGLT2 inhibitors were associated with CRC development. They found that high-dose semaglutide (2.4 mg injection weekly) was associated with a significant rise in CRC incidence (OR 1.61, 95% Cl 1.02-2.54), particularly among obese participants 57 . This fi nding raises the possibility of a dose-dependent effect, although it remains uncertain whether the signal represents an actual effect of semaglutide itself or if it is due to detection bias and a confounding variable of obesity severity.

A systematic review and meta-analysis of 7 large retrospective cohort studies including over 5 million individuals found an elevated relative risk of CRC among GLP-1RA users (RR 2.31, 95% CI 1.82 -2.93) 58 . However, this association lost significance when compared directly against other antidiabetic agents (OR 1.73, 95% CI 0.21 -14.18), suggesting other confounding variables were present. A Danish registry analysis with up to 10 years of follow-up compared those with treatment of GLP-1RA to DPP4 inhibitors. They identified a small increased risk in total cancer among longterm GLP-1RA users (HR 1.35, 95% CI 1.05 -1.73), but investigators thought this was due to survival bias. The study also notes possible confounding results due to BMI. 59

## Table 3

Studies Demonstrating an Increased Association Between Glucagon-like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonist Use and Colorectal Cancer

| First author   |   Year | Study design               | Population                                                                                   | Comparator                          | Main findings                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
|----------------|--------|----------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Silverii 56    |   2025 | Meta-analysis of 50 RCTs   | 102 772                                                                                      | Other antidiabetic drugs            | Increase in the risk for CRC (MH-OR 1.27 [1.03, 1.57]) was significant only in shorter-term trials up to 104 wk, (MH-OR 2.28 [1.04, 4.98]) but not over 104 wk (MH-OR 1.20 [0.96, 1.50])                                                                   |
| Hung 57        |   2025 | Meta-analysis of 68 RCTs   | 207 200                                                                                      | Placebo or other antidiabetic drugs | High-dose semaglutide (2.4 mg injection weekly) was associated with a rise in CRC incidence (OR 1.61, 95% Cl 1.02-2.54) highest among obese participants                                                                                                   |
| Zhong 58       |   2025 | Meta-analysis of 7 RCTs    | 5 066 681                                                                                    | Placebo or other antidiabetic drugs | In patients with T2DM, an elevated relative risk of CRC among GLP-1RA users (RR 2.31, 95% Cl 1.82-2.93) was demonstrated. However, this association lost significance when compared directly against other antidiabetic agents (OR 1.73, 95% CI0.21-14.18) |
| Gamborg 59     |   2025 | Retrospective cohort study | 39 460                                                                                       | DPP4- inhibitors                    | ADanish registry analysis with up to 10 years of follow-up demonstrated a small increased risk in total cancer among long-term GLP-1RA users (HR 1.35, 95% CI 1.05 - 1.73)                                                                                 |
| Sun 60         |   2024 | Mendelian randomization    | Participants from large-scale genome- wide association studies for 14 different cancer types | N/A                                 | Genetically-proxied GLP-1RA exposure was associated with increased CRC risk (OR 1.12, 95% Cl 1.07-1.18).                                                                                                                                                   |

Abbreviations: CRC, colorectal cancer; GLP-1RA, glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist; RCT, randomized control trial.

A Mendelian randomization study was combined with a systematic review of RCTs to examine whether GLP-1RAs impact cancer risk. The Mendelian randomization aspect of the study found that genetically-proxied GLP-1RA exposure was associated with increased CRC risk (OR 1.12, 95% CI 1.07 -1.18). The subsequent systematic review of 80 randomized controlled trials found a low overall incidence of malignancy among GLP-1RA users. 60

Overall, while most data suggests that GLP-1RAs are either neutral or protective against CRC, a small number of studies have suggested a potential increase in CRC risk.

## Conclusion

The relationship between GLP-1RAs and CRC is an area of ongoing research, with the available evidence presenting a complex picture. The majority of mechanistic studies suggest an antitumor and antimetastatic impact through metabolic reprogramming, suppression of oncogenic pathways, inhibition of angiogenesis and invasion, and other mechanisms. These fi ndings align with much of the real-world data, which generally shows either a neutral or protective association between GLP-1RA therapy and CRC risk.

Although a smaller number of studies have reported signals of increased CRC risk, these have largely been shown in select randomized trials, retrospective cohorts with considerable heterogeneity, and genetic analyses. Many of these fi ndings may reflect detection bias and confounding variables such as obesity rather than a true carcinogenic effect. Regardless, it still highlights the need for continued long-term surveillance as GLP-1RA use expands.

In total, the evidence suggests that GLP-1RAs are unlikely to meaningfully increase CRC risk and may in some contexts offer protective metabolic and anti-inflammatory benefits that reduce CRC incidence. There still remains a need for randomized controlled trials with long-term follow-up of 5 years or longer that are specifically designed to evaluate CRC outcomes. These studies would assess whether the dosage and duration of individual GLP1RAs have different impacts on CRC incidence. This would clarify whether certain agents or dosing regimens are consistently neutral, protective, or potentially harmful, and whether effects change over time.

As GLP-1RAs become increasingly central to the management of diabetes and obesity, understanding their long-term oncologic safety remains essential. Current data are largely reassuring. For most patients, the metabolic, cardiovascular, and weight-related benefits of GLP-1RA therapy appear to outweigh any theoretical cancer risk. Ongoing research will be critical in further understanding the risks and benefits of long-term GLP-1RA use.

## Disclosure

The authors have no conflicts of interest to disclose.
